title: Founder Mindset
body:
# Founder Mindset

Graham argues that the most important quality in a startup founder is determination. Not intelligence -- determination ([The Hardest Lessons for Startups to Learn](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8w-the-hardest-lessons-for-startups-to-learn)). Across several essays, he builds a picture of the founder mindset as a specific combination of relentlessness and flexibility.

## Relentlessly Resourceful

In the essay of that title, Graham proposes "relentlessly resourceful" as the two-word answer to what makes a good founder ([Be Relentlessly Resourceful](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/94-be-relentlessly-resourceful)). Not merely relentless -- that produces stubbornness. Not merely resourceful -- that produces aimlessness. The combination is what matters.

He uses the running back metaphor: a good running back does not just run straight ahead (relentless) or dance around (resourceful). They do both -- pushing forward while constantly adapting to the field. This same metaphor appears in "The Hardest Lessons."

Graham writes that "Make something people want" is the destination, but "Be relentlessly resourceful" is how you get there. He also argues this quality can be taught -- "surprisingly often it can. Not to everyone, but to many people." This resourcefulness is what enables the [[Early-Stage Tactics]] of doing things that don't scale.

## Determination Over Intelligence

Graham argues in "Why to not not start a startup" that determination is "probably the single best predictor of success" ([Why to not not start a startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8x-why-to-not-not-start-a-startup)). Most hackers underestimate their determination -- he has seen many become visibly more determined as they get used to running a startup. Founders who would have been delighted at first to sell for $2M become set on world domination.

He argues that intelligence requirements are modest. "If you're smart enough to worry that you might not be smart enough to start a startup, you probably are." Most companies do mundane work where effort, not brains, is the decisive factor. See also [[Becoming a Founder]] for Graham's full analysis of which reasons to hesitate are valid.

## Genuine Interest as the Real Driver

Graham's "Billionaires build" reveals that what keeps the most successful founders working long-term is not money but genuine interest in what they are building ([Billionaires build](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/9M-billionaires-build)). "One thing few people realize about billionaires is that all of them could have stopped sooner. They could have gotten acquired, or found someone else to run the company."

Scholl echoes this from the [[Biotech and Deep Tech|hard tech]] perspective: "Find something in the world that you desperately want to exist. Startups are hard -- all startups are hard -- and I think what makes the difference many times is are you working on something that is so meaningful to you, that it is worth it for you to go through hell to make it happen?" ([On starting and scaling aerospace company Boom](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/5Q-on-starting-and-scaling-aerospace-company-boom)).

Founders driven primarily by money will take the first large acquisition offer. Founders doing it to seem cool will find less painful ways to seem cool. The ones who succeed at the largest scale are building their project.

## Counterintuitive Nature of Startups

"Before the Startup" identifies six ways startups are counterintuitive ([Before the Startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8y-before-the-startup)). The most important: the way to succeed is not to be an expert on startups but to be an expert on users and the problem. "Starting a startup is where gaming the system stops working. There is no boss to trick, only users."

Graham warns specifically about "playing house" -- going through the motions of having a startup (office, business cards, [[Hiring]]) without doing the hard work of making something people want. This connects to his advice in [[Startup Ideas]] that domain expertise matters more than startup mechanics.

## What Actually Kills Startups

Graham's "18 Mistakes" identifies the single meta-mistake: not making something users want ([The 18 Mistakes That Kill Startups](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8t-the-18-mistakes-that-kill-startups)). "Nearly all failure funnels through that." The most common form of startup death is not a spectacular flameout but a half-hearted side project that never gets anywhere (#18: half-hearted effort).

In "The Hardest Lessons," he identifies three self-inflicted causes of death: internal disputes between [[Cofounders]], inertia (not releasing, not iterating), and ignoring users ([The Hardest Lessons for Startups to Learn](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8w-the-hardest-lessons-for-startups-to-learn)). Competitors almost never kill startups -- startups kill themselves.

## Optimism and Deals

Graham treats founder optimism as a nuclear reactor: enormously powerful but dangerous if unshielded. Founders should be optimistic about their ability to build something great, but cynical about deals, partnerships, and anything that requires other people to follow through ([The Hardest Lessons for Startups to Learn](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8w-the-hardest-lessons-for-startups-to-learn)). "The most dangerous thing is to let them go to your head." This skepticism is especially important during [[Fundraising]] and [[Sales and Distribution]] negotiations.

## Formidable

In the [[Fundraising]] context, Graham describes the quality investors look for as "formidable" -- not just confident, but justifiably confident ([How to Convince Investors](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/98-how-to-convince-investors)). The best way for inexperienced founders to be formidable is to stick to the truth about what they know and what they have built.

## What Makes Unicorn Founders Different

Livingston identifies nine traits that separate massively successful founders from merely successful ones ([What's different about unicorns](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8L-what-s-different-about-unicorns)). Luck and timing come first -- the best ideas grow organically from founders' lives, not from deliberate planning. Beyond luck, the unicorn founders are never primarily motivated by money or status. They are "fanatically interested in what the company is doing." They are independent-minded, ambitious enough to embrace a frighteningly large opportunity rather than shrink from it, and possess exceptional drive that may not be visible until the authority vacuum of a startup reveals it.

Livingston adds a critical late-stage requirement: the founder must become a manager. "Designing cool products and managing people are really different things. It is a rare person who can be great at both. But you have to be if you want to create one of the really big startups." See [[Management]] for the maker-to-manager transition.

## Resilience Over Confidence

Taggar, after working with nearly a thousand founders, concludes that resilience is the quality that matters most ([Should you start a startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/GV-should-you-start-a-startup)). He initially used confidence as a proxy for resilience but found this wrong -- some outwardly confident founders crumbled at the first setback, while quiet founders like Sajith of Benchling (now valued at $6B+) endured years of rejection to transform into formidable leaders.

Taggar argues that no simple test predicts resilience. Neither school performance nor work success reliably correlates with startup resilience. The practical advice: assess whether you can live with the worst-case scenario (typically a year of low or no salary). "Be honest with yourself. If you can't actually handle the worst case, then your own constant anxiety will probably self-sabotage your startup efforts."

## Never Lose Momentum

Altman argues that one of a founder's most important jobs is never losing momentum ([How to succeed with a startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/89-how-to-succeed-with-a-startup)). "If you have momentum, people keep delivering results beyond what they think they're capable of. If you lose momentum, it's very difficult to get it back." The startup should maintain a cadence of winning on short, predictable intervals. This means the first few years offer no real work-life balance -- a reality YC tries to be honest about.

Altman also describes the team qualities that sustain momentum: optimists who maintain internal conviction when the world says they will fail, idea generators who constantly propose new approaches (most bad, some essential), and people with a "we'll figure it out" spirit who act with incomplete data rather than waiting for certainty.

## Conviction

Caldwell and Seibel identify conviction as a distinct, trainable founder trait ([Building confidence in yourself and your ideas](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Kg-building-confidence-in-yourself-and-your-ideas)). A founder with low conviction -- who pivots after 25 cold emails go unanswered, or abandons an idea because investors say no -- will never build anything. Conviction does not mean religious belief; it means not getting "blown off course" by every piece of negative feedback.

"The most important customer is yourself" -- the first step in building a company is convincing your own brain that the idea is worth working on. Founders who skip this step do what Caldwell calls a "random walk" -- changing direction at random intervals, never accumulating forward progress, and running out of energy in 1.5-2 years with nothing to show for it.

Fear is the primary cause of bad thinking. Founders fabricate benchmarks ("75% of the batch has already launched"), accept anonymous internet advice as gospel, and let fundraising anxiety override domain expertise. YC's $500K standard deal was designed partly to reduce this desperation.

## Don't Think Like an Investor

Caldwell and Seibel warn founders against applying investor frameworks -- market sizing, competitive analysis, exit strategies -- to early-stage decisions ([Why founders shouldn't think like investors](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Kk-why-founders-shouldn-t-think-like-investors)). Founders who think like VCs tend to choose ideas based on what raises money rather than what solves problems, spend time on macro analysis instead of getting customers, and filter out ideas that seem too small. The cure: "Turn off the pollution" of investor content, spend time with users who do not care about fundraising trends, and build expertise in a specific domain rather than analyzing markets from an apartment.

## When to Trust Your Gut

Caldwell and Seibel distinguish between founders with expertise and founders without ([When should you trust your gut?](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Kw-when-should-you-trust-your-gut)). A founder who spent ten years building mortgage underwriting software should trust their instincts about what mortgage underwriters need. A founder who just bought a house and has ideas about real estate should not. The common failure: the expert is fearful and acts like the non-expert, while the non-expert is overconfident and acts like the expert.

## Stop Innovating on the Wrong Things

Caldwell and Seibel argue that startups have a limited amount of "innovation juice" and should concentrate it entirely on making something people want ([Stop innovating on the wrong things](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Kc-stop-innovating-on-the-wrong-things)). Common anti-patterns include: innovating on corporate structure (Wyoming LLCs, nonprofit-owns-for-profit), choosing idiosyncratic programming languages, inventing novel [[Pricing|pricing]] models that confuse customers, and trying to disprove all startup advice simultaneously. Seibel frames it: "Making a startup work is a miracle. The odds that you are going to be able to perform five miracles at once is much, much, much lower."

## How Billion-Dollar Startups Start

YC partners examining the origins of top companies find consistent patterns ([How do billion dollar startups start?](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Li-how-do-billion-dollar-startups-start)). The companies looked ordinary at the start -- Solugen brought a beaker of hydrogen peroxide to their interview and sold bottles from a garage. Amplitude applied with a voice-to-text Android app that the partners did not believe in, but funded because of the founder's intensity. Brex came in with a VR startup. Segment pitched classroom polling software with zero traction.

Seibel identifies a quality he calls "obstinance" in the best founders: "Whatever they're working on, they don't do it at 60 or 70 or even 80 percent. They're always just 100 percent committed." Diana Hu adds that the best early-stage founders are "not well-rounded. They're very quirky people."

## The Asymmetric Upside of Optimism

Caldwell and Seibel argue that optimism is not naive -- it is asymmetric ([The asymmetric upside of being positive](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/KS-the-asymmetric-upside-of-being-positive)). They catalog advances since the 1990s -- information access, communications, transportation, healthcare, entertainment -- and note that none of them seemed transformative at the time. Pessimism is rewarded on social media and feels sophisticated, but it produces nothing. Builders must be optimistic to invest years in a project.

Seibel frames it directly: "If you really care about those problems, you have to be optimistic to try to solve them." The snarky critic who never builds anything is nobody's hero. Caldwell adds that the greatest change agents in history were default optimistic -- "they believed effort wasn't a waste of time."

This connects to the broader theme of [[Financial Survival|balancing optimism with realism]] -- founders must believe the future is worth building while acknowledging current constraints.

## Confronting Reality

Garry Tan describes the danger of the opposite extreme: living in a "dreamland" where the founder avoids hard truths ([The Main Function: How to go from startup dream to reality](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/JG-the-main-function-how-to-go-from-startup-dream-to-reality)). At Posterous, two years of 20-30% monthly growth went flat, and Tan looked for statistics that confirmed they were still fine rather than confronting the competitive shift. He compares this to The Truman Show -- the founder's constructed reality.

The solution is not to abandon the dream but to bridge it with reality. Tan cites Ray Dalio: "Whatever success I've had in life has had to do more with my ability to know how to deal with my not knowing." Surrounding yourself with smart, independent thinkers who challenge groupthink -- not yes-men -- converts individual dreamland into collective reality. Karl Popper's falsifiability principle applies: founders discover truth through experimentation and criticism, not certainty.

## Perseverance Through Crisis

Edrizio De La Cruz's path from selling guavas in Santo Domingo to selling his fintech company Arcus to Mastercard illustrates perseverance under extreme conditions ([From Underdog to Undefeated](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/KG-from-underdog-to-undefeated-the-edrizio-de-la-cruz-founder-story)). After 144 investor meetings with no results for his Series A, the one term sheet he received collapsed four weeks before running out of cash. He flew to Mexico for a backup meeting, survived an earthquake that cancelled it, refused to leave, delivered his first-ever pitch in Spanish, and raised $8M before running out of money.

His guiding principle: "You got to stick around long enough to get lucky. Just don't die." During COVID, he was voted out of his own company temporarily, experienced a personal miscarriage, and faced a stalled Series B -- then won a Visa competition and used the momentum to raise from SoftBank and Citi, eventually attracting Mastercard's acquisition. De La Cruz credits Marcus Aurelius: "The impediment to action advances action. What stands in the way becomes the way."

## Riding Exponentials and Platform Shifts

Buchheit, reflecting on Gmail and his time at Google and Facebook, argues that every company worth $100B+ is sitting on top of an exponential change in the world ([The path to $100B](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/82-the-path-to-100b)). Intel and Microsoft rode the microcomputer wave. Google rode the internet's information explosion. The founder's job is to identify a change that will be fundamentally different in ten years and position the company to capture it.

Buchheit describes founder conviction as almost irrational. Zuckerberg turned down Yahoo's $1B offer when Facebook had 10 million users and replaced the management team that pressured him to sell. When 8 million of 10 million users protested the News Feed, he held firm. Musk invested his entire fortune in three rockets that all blew up, then scraped together enough for a fourth. "That is insane. I would not take all of my money and plough it into some harebrained rocket scheme. That's a level of irrationality that is I think wonderful."

## Surviving Cyclical Industries

Chandan Lodha of CoinTracker describes building a company through crypto's boom-bust cycles, where "the entire world is telling us that the entire space that we're working in is irrelevant, dead, and doesn't make sense" ([How to survive the crypto boom & bust cycle](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Km-how-to-survive-the-crypto-boom-bust-cycle)). Six months from shutting down in 2020, CoinTracker survived by having an excellent cofounder relationship, returning to first principles about mission, and being transparent with the team about the company's financial reality. The company grew 14x during the 2021 bull run, then used the subsequent bear market to completely rebuild their product over nine months. CoinTracker shifted a guiding principle from "think long-term" to "think long-term, ship today" -- a small change that "gave everyone in the company the permission to take risks and experiment."

## Beware of Startup Experts

Caldwell and Seibel warn that bad startup advice is worse than no advice ([The problem with startup experts](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/LM-the-problem-with-startup-experts)). Local startup ecosystems, university innovation labs, and corporate accelerators often have incentives misaligned with founder success -- they exist to create local jobs or hit quarterly KPIs, not to build billion-dollar companies. "No startup advice is better than bad startup advice." Founders should source advice from people who have actually started successful companies, not from people who studied or invested in them.

## Beginner's Mind and the Midwit Trap

Caldwell and Seibel use the "midwit meme" to illustrate how overthinking destroys startups ([Elon Musk and the midwit meme](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/IW-dalton-michael-elon-musk-and-the-midwit-meme)). In the meme, the genius and the beginner reach the same conclusion, while the person with intermediate knowledge overthinks their way into paralysis. "What would an idiot do?" is a surprisingly reliable heuristic for the correct action.

They apply this to fundraising (raise what you need and get back to work), MVPs (build fast and ship), [[Cofounders|cofounder]] selection (work with your friend), and [[Product-Market Fit]] (you know it when you cannot sleep). The exception: "trap door decisions" involving law, regulation, or people's health require careful thought. For everything else, the founders who move fastest tend to win.

## Give More Than You Take

Caldwell and Seibel argue that the core of a good startup is creating more value than it captures ([How generosity built tech giants](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/IO-dalton-michael-how-generosity-built-tech-giants)). Google Ads made early advertisers far more money than they paid Google. Excel made office workers 10-100x more productive. The founders who fear being "consultants" and avoid deeply understanding customer problems end up building products nobody wants.

The historical context reinforces the point: Linux, GCC, SQLite, HTML, and web browsers were all created by people giving away value. "If you leave a lot of the value on the table for your customers instead of trying to take it all, you're in the game."

## Zero-Sum vs. Positive-Sum Thinking

Caldwell and Seibel distinguish founders playing positive-sum games (building products that create new value) from those playing zero-sum games (capturing value from others) ([Game theory and startups](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Ib-dalton-michael-what-basic-game-theory-teaches-us-about-startups)). The positive-sum founder's company keeps running even when markets drop. The zero-sum player -- dependent on rising asset prices, leverage, or information asymmetry -- collapses when the music stops.

Seibel frames FOMO as the primary temptation: seeing others get rich from zero-sum games while you build houses. The antidote is asking whether what you are building would survive a downturn. "If you're solving someone's real problem and the economy goes down, it's not like they still don't have that problem."

## Setting Goals That Matter

Caldwell and Seibel identify common goal-setting failures ([Setting smarter goals](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Id-dalton-michael-setting-smarter-goals)). Goals that are too aggressive become demotivating. Fake metrics (registered users instead of revenue, downloads instead of active users) create false confidence. Sandbagging -- setting easy goals to look impressive -- is rational in a big company but self-defeating when you are your own boss.

The "play stupid games, win stupid prizes" framework applies: raising as much money as possible wins the prize of losing board control and being unable to pivot. Hiring 50 employees wins the prize of painful layoffs. Scaling negative margins wins the prize of investors who stop returning calls. The goal should be to work on the hardest problem in the company, not to hit a number that impresses someone else.

## Founder Health and Lifestyle

Caldwell and Seibel argue that founders must "adult harder" than their peers ([Lifestyle habits of successful founders](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Ii-dalton-michael-most-important-lifestyle-habits-of-successful-founders)). Sleep, diet, exercise, and sunlight form the 80/20 of preventative mental health. Social media consumption is an information diet that affects mood as much as food affects the body -- successful founders aggressively limit it.

When setbacks occur, the first question should be: "Do I have to do anything right now?" Sleeping on it is the minimum response. "No action" is one of the best founders' most-used plays -- 20-40% of the time, the right move is to do nothing. The best people respond to crises by hiking; the worst respond by drinking. Therapy and medication are destigmatized tools that many successful founders use but rarely discuss publicly.

For fatal punches -- when the startup truly fails -- Caldwell and Seibel tell founders: "Your startup failed. That doesn't mean you failed." The experience has value even if the company does not survive. But founders should not sacrifice their savings, families, or mental health past the point of recovery.

## The CEO Role Evolves Every Six Months

Houston describes the founder transition from coder to CEO as a series of identity crises. "Nobody tells you that your job as CEO changes every six months." At 20 people, you manage by intuition. At 50, that breaks. Houston recommends asking: "One year from now, what will I wish I had been learning?" He credits reading -- High Output Management (Andy Grove), The Effective Executive (Drucker), Poor Charlie's Almanack (Munger) -- and maintaining relationships with founders at different stages ([On starting and scaling Dropbox](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/6S-on-starting-and-scaling-dropbox-yc-w07)).

Greene, who cofounded three startups including VMware ($19.1B market cap at IPO), uses a sailing metaphor: "Building a company is so much like racing a sailboat. You need a plan and then the ability to reevaluate the plan in real time." At VMware, she learned to make innovation non-disruptive to the established order. At Bebop, she learned that tenaciousness pays off when the initial design is wrong ([Diane Greene's advice for founders](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/6Q-diane-greene-s-advice-for-founders)).

Collison distinguishes happiness from fulfillment. Running Stripe was "not especially happy" -- constant awareness of product deficiencies and investor skepticism. But it felt fulfilling. He recommends optimizing for fulfillment, while noting genuine unhappiness is a signal to reconsider ([Patrick Collison on effectively running a startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/80-patrick-collison-on-effectively-running-a-startup)).

## Customer Obsession as Identity

Xu describes DoorDash's founding as following a thread discovered by watching a macaroon shop owner's daily life -- a booklet of turned-down delivery orders. The founders validated by doing deliveries themselves, a practice the entire company still performs annually twelve years later ([How to build the future: Tony Xu](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/MJ-how-to-build-the-future-tony-xu)).

Lieb describes persistence through a different lens: Bump reached 150 million users but could not find a business model. Rather than quitting, he pivoted, got acquired by Google, and built Google Photos -- one billion users in four years. "The path to success is very rarely a straight line" ([How David Lieb turned a failing startup into Google Photos](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Ly-how-david-lieb-turned-a-failing-startup-into-google-photos)).

Heller at Casetext spent ten years building AI legal tools. When large language models arrived, a decade of domain expertise let Casetext compress weeks of legal work into minutes. Sold for $650 million ([The 10-year overnight success story of Casetext](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/KJ-the-10-year-overnight-success-story-of-casetext)).

## Competitors Are Not the Threat

Seibel, Taggar, and Flora address founder obsession with competitors. The "most depressing fact" is that most likely everyone dies -- both you and your competitors fail. The questions that matter: are customers talking about the competitor, are you losing deals, and how does the product compare? ([How to compete with Amazon and Google](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Iz-inside-the-group-partner-lounge-how-to-compete-with-amazon-and-google))

## Productivity as Survival

YC partners frame productivity as the core survival skill. The primary failure mode: spending time on accessible, fun tasks rather than the drudgery that moves the business. The fix: write down priorities, audit the calendar, resist tools that impose someone else's mental model ([Productivity tips](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/K5-yc-s-group-partners-share-productivity-tips-and-advice-on-actually-getting-things-done)).

## Irrational Optimism Plus Uncompromising Realism

Mohan (Windsurf CEO) describes the dual mindset required for a successful [[Pivoting|pivot]]: "irrational optimism because if you don't have the optimism, you just won't do anything," combined with "uncompromising realism, which is that when the facts change, you actually change your mind" ([Windsurf CEO](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/MO-windsurf-ceo-betting-on-ai-agents-pivoting-in-48-hours-and-the-future-of-coding)). He pivoted from a profitable GPU infrastructure company to AI coding over a weekend. The tension between these two qualities is precisely what makes execution hard: "the thing that makes you succeed through irrational optimism is exactly opposite to the things that allow you to be a very realistic company."

Mohan also articulates a philosophy of depreciating insights: "Every single insight that we have is a depreciating insight. If we don't continually have insights that we are executing on, we are just slowly dying." He is comfortable with 50% of bets failing: if 100% succeed, the company is not trying hard enough.

## Build for the Love of Building

Palicha (Zepto CEO, age 22) advises his 17-year-old self: "Really do it for the love of building. The end goal should not be I build so I can make XYZ. I'm building so that I have the opportunity to wake up in the morning and build again" ([How Zepto became India's fastest growing startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/ML-how-zepto-became-india-s-fastest-growing-startup)). Zepto went from a WhatsApp group to $3B+ GMV and $5B valuation.

Pei (Nothing founder) echoes from hardware: "There is a beauty to not having another option. It forces you to survive" ([Nothing founder Carl Pei](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/LO-how-nothing-founder-carl-pei-built-a-multi-million-dollar-smartphone-brand-in-just-2-years)). When 90% of his first earbud shipment failed, he put 15 engineers on the factory floor until quality recovered. Nothing reached $600M annualized revenue within two years.

## Follow the Scaling Line

Truell (Cursor CEO) describes the conviction that drove Cursor: "follow the line" -- plan for where the scaling laws predict models will be, not where they are today ([Cursor CEO](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/MU-cursor-ceo-going-beyond-code-superintelligent-ai-agents-and-why-taste-still-matters)). He warns against optimizing for the demo. The Cursor team dogfooded relentlessly and tracked paid power users rather than vanity metrics. They hired extremely slowly: "If you really nail the first 10 people, they will both accelerate you in the future" and act as an immune system against bad hires.

## The Long Game and Burnout

Altman argues that very few founders make an actual long-term commitment. Those who plan for a 10-year journey have a huge advantage ([Later stage advice for startups](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4q-later-stage-advice-for-startups)). But the long game requires sustainability. Founders who go three or four years without vacation will burn out. Altman advises monitoring the team for burnout, encouraging vacation, and providing new challenges.

As success grows, so does criticism. "People start rooting against you. Journalists and people on the internet will find ways to pick apart what you're doing." Learning to ignore detractors early is essential. Losing focus -- to burnout, to potential acquirers, or to distractions -- destroys the advantage that focus created in the first place.

title: Company Culture
body:
# Company Culture

Brady defines company culture as the implicit set of behaviors inside a company — what employees do when no one has told them what to do. Done right, the right behaviors support a good business in intangible ways over the life of the company ([Building culture](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/6r-building-culture)).

## Why It Matters Early

The first ~20 employees are the company's cultural DNA. These people will hire and train the next wave when the company scales after [[Product-Market Fit]]. If the early employees embody the right values, the culture propagates. If they do not, mistakes compound and become nearly impossible to correct later. "If you think you're gonna wake up at 100 employees and then start a diversity program, you're fooling yourself" ([Building culture](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/6r-building-culture)).

## Six Things Founders Can Do Now

Brady's six-step framework for early-stage culture-building ([Building culture](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/6r-building-culture)):

**1. Be proud of the problem you are solving.** If founders chose the idea with their ego — because it sounds impressive at parties — they will lack the energy to sustain the company through hard times. Everyone around the founders will see how they feel. That emotional signal sets the tone for culture.

**2. Create a long-term vision that others will follow.** The vision should describe purpose, not the product. Tesla: "To accelerate the world's transition to sustainable energy" (no mention of cars). Microsoft: "A computer on every desk in every home" (crazy talk in the early 1980s). Google: "Organize the world's information and make it universally accessible and useful" (no mention of a search engine). These visions attract people who identify with the mission.

**3. Define values as a hiring filter.** Have a conversation with your [[Cofounders|cofounder]] about the behaviors you want inside the company. Keep the list to fewer than five items. At this stage, it is informal — the seed of a future corporate values list. Examples: Spotify ("Innovative. Collaborative. Sincere. Passionate. Playful.") and Atlassian ("Open company, no bullshit. Don't fuck with the customer. Play as a team.").

**4. Make values externally focused.** Culture statements focused on the customer provide better behavioral guidance than internally-focused ones. Brady contrasts Facebook's "Move fast and break things" (internally focused, no guidance on what not to break — "Shouldn't be a surprise when you look at this that some of the privacy violations have occurred") with Google's "Don't be evil" (externally focused, providing guardrails that protected user trust).

**5. Have a conversation about diversity.** Not just ethnic and gender diversity, but diversity of opinions. Research suggests that companies fostering strong, opposed opinions are more creative. The natural tendency in early [[Hiring|hiring]] — tapping friends and former colleagues — produces homogeneity. Founders should discuss diversity explicitly before it becomes an afterthought.

**6. Put a hiring plan in place.** From the first employee, follow a structured process that incorporates the values conversation. Assess whether the process is working after the first few hires. Plan to evolve it before scale-up requires it. See [[Hiring]] for more on the hiring process.

## Culture and Prioritization

Seibel warns that many founders "cargo cult" the startup — prioritizing press, conferences, [[Hiring|hiring]], and investors over getting product to users. "You are pretending to be a startup, but the actual work of startups is pushing product, getting it in the users' hands, and seeing if they like it" ([Biggest mistakes first-time founders make](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/66-biggest-mistakes-first-time-founders-make)). This prioritization failure is itself a culture problem: what founders model in the early days becomes the implicit behavior of the company.

## The Cargo Culting Problem

Caldwell and Seibel dedicate an entire episode to cargo culting -- superficially copying successful companies without understanding why they did what they did ([Silicon Valley's cargo culting problem](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/IR-dalton-michael-silicon-valley-s-cargo-culting-problem)). Classic examples: copying Google's open offices, free snacks, and flat org structure without recognizing that Google hired brilliant engineers to solve a genuinely hard technical problem. Copying Facebook's "don't charge users, build an ads business" model without having a product people use two hours per day. Copying Uber's aggressive expansion without having unit economics working in a single city.

They identify three levels of cargo culting. Classic: copying a successful company's surface features. Modern: copying a company that raised money but has not succeeded (copying WeWork or Zumi Robotics based on fundraising announcements). Super-modern: copying another struggling startup because they "look like a startup."

Caldwell uses a plagiarism analogy: the difference between copying three papers into your report and reading broadly, digesting, and synthesizing original work. "The thing that sucks about startups is that you do have to think about a lot of things. There isn't just a checklist." The antidote is starting from the user's perspective: what do they actually care about? Users care that Google returns good search results, not about its logo styling. Users care that Uber arrives in five minutes, not how many cities it operates in.

## The Airbnb Approach

Chesky describes how Airbnb wrote its core values before hiring a single employee -- a process that took roughly six months. The first engineer took four to five months to find because Chesky screened for cultural fit alongside technical ability. His reasoning: the first engineer is "like bringing in DNA to your company. There were going to be a thousand people just like him or her" ([Building company culture](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/JN-how-to-start-a-startup-building-company-culture-part-i)).

Airbnb uses dedicated core values interviewers -- people outside the functional team being hired for -- to assess cultural alignment separately from technical skill. Chesky personally interviewed the first 300 employees. His screening question evolved from "if you had a year left to live, would you take this job?" (too extreme) to the ten-year version. He wanted people on a calling, not looking for a job: "There is a job and then there's a calling."

Lin (former Zappos COO) provides the theoretical framework. Culture is "the everyday core values and actions of each member of the team in pursuit of the company mission." He introduces Patrick Lencioni's five dysfunctions pyramid: teams break down from lack of trust, which prevents healthy conflict, which prevents genuine commitment, which prevents accountability, which prevents results. Culture that builds trust at the foundation enables everything above it.

Lin and Chesky both emphasize that culture does not pay off in the short term. Chesky states: "If you wanted to in one year build a company and sell it as quickly as possible, the number one piece of advice I give you is screw the culture." Culture is an investment that compounds over years, and the founding DNA -- the stories, habits, and decisions from the earliest days -- propagate through the organization as it scales.

## Missionaries vs. Mercenaries

Chesky applies cultural thinking to strategic decisions. When the Samwer brothers cloned Airbnb in 2011 with $90 million and 400 employees (vs. Airbnb's 40 employees and $7 million), the pragmatic move was to acquire them. Chesky refused because "we were missionaries and they were mercenaries." The decision was controversial but vindicated: Airbnb now generates more than 50% of revenue from Europe.

## Source Talks

| Source | Speaker | Key Point |
|--------|---------|-----------|
| [Building culture](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/6r-building-culture) | Tim Brady | Culture as behavior, first 20 as DNA, six-step framework |
| [Biggest mistakes first-time founders make](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/66-biggest-mistakes-first-time-founders-make) | Michael Seibel | Cargo culting startup behaviors, prioritize product over sizzle |
| [Building company culture](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/JN-how-to-start-a-startup-building-company-culture-part-i) | Brian Chesky, Alfred Lin | Values before first hire, core values interviewers, missionaries vs. mercenaries |
| [Silicon Valley's cargo culting problem](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/IR-dalton-michael-silicon-valley-s-cargo-culting-problem) | Dalton Caldwell, Michael Seibel | Three levels of cargo culting; plagiarism analogy; start from the user |

title: Product-Market Fit
body:
# Product-Market Fit

Product-market fit is the state where a product satisfies strong market demand. Rusenko calls it "the top problem that anyone deals with" and ranks it as the hardest challenge in a startup, ahead of [[Hiring|hiring a world-class team]] and far ahead of monetization ([How to find product-market fit](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/7V-how-to-find-product-market-fit)). Seibel describes PMF more viscerally: "If you're asking, you don't have it." When PMF arrives, the founder stops thinking about new features and distribution and instead struggles just to keep the product online for the users flooding in ([How to plan an MVP](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/6f-how-to-plan-an-mvp)).

## Measuring PMF with Retention

Alstromer argues that retention is the single best, least biased measure of product-market fit ([Growth for startups](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/6k-growth-for-startups)). The method: identify the metric that represents the core value of the product (bookings for Airbnb, rides for Lyft, payroll runs for Gusto), determine the natural frequency (daily, weekly, monthly, annual), then plot a cohort retention curve. If the curve flattens — meaning some percentage of users continues returning indefinitely — the product has PMF for those users. If the curve trends to zero, it does not.

DoorDash retained 21% of users 20 months after signup. GitHub retained 30% after 60 months. Both indicate strong PMF. Net Promoter Score, surveys, registered user counts, visitor counts, and conversion rates are all poor substitutes — they do not capture whether users come back ([Growth for startups](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/6k-growth-for-startups)).

A key distinction: if a product is expected to charge money, free usage does not demonstrate PMF. "If someone says, 'I love this if it's free, but if it costs money, I'm not gonna use it,' that's pretty bad" (Alstromer). See [[Pricing]] for related advice on charging early.

## The Timeline to PMF

Rusenko documents Weebly's path: 14 months in, the user graph was trending downward despite press in TechCrunch and Newsweek. At 18 months, still no PMF. The inflection came at 20 months, when organic daily signups began exceeding what any press spike had generated ([How to find product-market fit](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/7V-how-to-find-product-market-fit)). This timeline has implications for [[Financial Survival]]: if it takes 20-30 iterations, founders must keep burn low enough to survive that many cycles.

Rusenko estimates that however many iterations a founder expects it will take, they should multiply by ten. This means outsourcing development rarely works — outsourcing buys one shot, but PMF requires twenty or thirty ([How to find product-market fit](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/7V-how-to-find-product-market-fit)).

## How to Find PMF

Rusenko breaks the process into a literal loop ([How to find product-market fit](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/7V-how-to-find-product-market-fit)):

1. Talk to customers and develop a market thesis
2. Listen to their problems, not their solutions
3. Rapid prototype and user-test
4. Build a solution to their problems
5. Test the solution with them
6. If it did not work, return to step 1

He emphasizes UX testing sessions as the most important tool: sit users in front of the product, ask them to perform a task, and do not touch the keyboard or speak until they complete it. Three to five sessions reveal the most critical usability failures.

Seibel adds a problem-definition checklist: Can you state the problem in two sentences? Have you experienced it yourself? Can you define it narrowly? Is it solvable? ([Building product](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/7s-building-product)). He recommends targeting desperate customers first — "whose business is gonna go out of business without using you?" — rather than impressive but non-desperate prospects.

## The Uncanny Valley of PMF

Caldwell identifies a dangerous middle zone: a startup with a little traction but no real PMF. A complete failure frees founders to pivot immediately. A small amount of traction traps them. "I've seen a little bit of traction be a trap that has captured a lot of really talented people for long periods of time" ([All about pivoting](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/6p-all-about-pivoting)). See [[Pivoting]] for more on recognizing when to change ideas.

## Creating Markets

Rusenko notes that the best companies create their own market — Airbnb, iPhone, Weebly. By definition, market research cannot identify a market that does not yet exist. The pattern: find a hidden need, build a product that initially looks like a worse version of something in an existing category, but actually serves a new population of users entirely ([How to find product-market fit](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/7V-how-to-find-product-market-fit)). The iPhone did not compete with existing smartphones. It made everyone a smartphone user.

## Minimum Remarkable Product

Rusenko prefers "minimum remarkable product" over "minimum viable product." The standard: launch when the product is better than what is currently available. Not perfect — but better. Paul Buchheit's formulation: "Launch when your product is better than what's out there" ([How to find product-market fit](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/7V-how-to-find-product-market-fit)). See [[Launching]] and [[Early-Stage Tactics]] for more on launch timing.

## Source Talks

| Source | Speaker | Key Point |
|--------|---------|-----------|
| [How to find product-market fit](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/7V-how-to-find-product-market-fit) | David Rusenko | Weebly journey, 20-month timeline, UX testing, minimum remarkable product |
| [Growth for startups](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/6k-growth-for-startups) | Gustaf Alströmer | Retention as PMF measure, cohort curves, bad PMF metrics |
| [Building product](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/7s-building-product) | Michael Seibel | Problem definition, desperate customers, charging early |
| [How to plan an MVP](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/6f-how-to-plan-an-mvp) | Michael Seibel | PMF as overwhelming demand, iterate vs. pivot |
| [All about pivoting](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/6p-all-about-pivoting) | Dalton Caldwell | Uncanny valley of PMF, little traction as a trap |

title: Board Governance
body:
# Board Governance

## Board Meetings

Hariharan argues that the biggest mistake is treating board meetings as status updates. Best CEOs spend only the first 45 minutes on KPIs and allocate the majority of time to 1-2 strategic deep-dives on the hardest problems ([How to create and manage a board](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/3w-how-to-create-and-manage-a-board)).

Written memos (pioneered by companies like Segment and Faire) are superior to slides for strategic board discussion. They force clearer thinking and allow board members to engage with nuance rather than reacting to bullet points ([How to create and manage a board](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/3w-how-to-create-and-manage-a-board)).

## Building Board Relationships

The #1 attribute needed in a board member is trust, and the only way to build it is time. Hariharan recommends getting to know VC partners 6-9 months before [[Fundraising|fundraising]] begins ([How to create and manage a board](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/3w-how-to-create-and-manage-a-board)).

Monthly 2-page update emails accomplish three things: board members can help with specific asks, quarterly meetings are better-prepared, and the CEO gains a forcing function for reflection. This practice ties into broader [[Management|management]] discipline.

## Board Control

Kwon and Harris warn that the most significant way founders lose control at Series A is the 2-2-1 board structure (2 founders, 2 investors, 1 independent) — because it means founders can be fired from their own company. A 2-1 structure preserves founder control ([A standard and clean Series A term sheet](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4P-a-standard-and-clean-series-a-term-sheet)).

"When an investor says that they're committed to partnering with you for the long-term but then tells you something else with the terms they insist on, believe the terms." Series A documents set precedent for future rounds — bad terms compound and are difficult to unwind ([A standard and clean Series A term sheet](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4P-a-standard-and-clean-series-a-term-sheet)). This connects directly to [[Financial Survival]] and long-term [[Fundraising]] strategy.

## Source Talks

| Source | Speaker | Key Point |
|--------|---------|-----------|
| [How to create and manage a board](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/3w-how-to-create-and-manage-a-board) | Anu Hariharan | Board meetings as strategy sessions, written memos, monthly updates |
| [A standard and clean Series A term sheet](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4P-a-standard-and-clean-series-a-term-sheet) | Jason Kwon, Aaron Harris | Board control, clean vs. dirty terms, believe the terms |

title: Becoming a Founder
body:
# Becoming a Founder

Multiple YC sources address the pre-founding question: should you start a startup, and when? Graham, Seibel, and YC staff collectively address 16+ reasons people hesitate, what college students should do to prepare, and how the founder path compares to alternatives.

## Dismantling Excuses

Graham's "Why to not not start a startup" systematically evaluates 16 reasons people don't start companies ([Why to not not start a startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8x-why-to-not-not-start-a-startup)). Most are not valid:

**Too young.** The lower bound may be as low as 16. Sam Altman was 19 when YC funded him. Graham identifies two tests of adult readiness: whether you've lost the "kid flake reflex" (crying "I can't" to get out of hard things), and whether you respond to challenges as an equal rather than from a position of submission.

**Too inexperienced.** Graham reversed his earlier view that founders should be at least 23. "The best way to get experience if you're 21 is to start a startup." A normal job may actually reduce startup readiness by turning people into "tame animals."

**Not smart enough.** "If you're smart enough to worry that you might not be smart enough to start a startup, you probably are." Most startups require effort more than intelligence.

**Know nothing about business.** "You don't need to know anything about business to start a startup." The initial focus is product. Revenue follows from making something people want.

**No idea.** 70% of YC startup ideas change in the first three months. YC cares more about builders than ideas. See [[Startup Ideas]].

The valid reasons: having a family to support (#9), being independently wealthy and unmotivated (#10), and not being ready for a multi-year commitment (#11).

Graham frames the broader argument historically: jobs as the default way to make a living are only about a hundred years old. Startups may represent another shift comparable to the move from farming to manufacturing.

## The Student Advantage

Graham identifies five advantages young founders have: stamina, poverty, rootlessness, access to [[Cofounders|cofounders]], and ignorance ([A student's guide to startups](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/93-a-student-s-guide-to-startups)).

**Poverty** is an advantage because living cheaply reduces burn rate. The Apple II was cheap because Wozniak designed it for himself and couldn't afford anything more. Low cost creates products that are affordable and therefore popular.

**Cofounders access** is the scarcest resource. School concentrates smart, ambitious, unattached people. By 30, potential cofounders are tied to jobs and families. Google, Yahoo, and Microsoft were all founded by people who met in school.

**Ignorance** acts as a counterweight to intimidation. Wozniak: "All the best things that I did at Apple came from (a) not having money and (b) not having done it before, ever."

The biggest disadvantage of student founders: building things that look like class projects. Graham identifies two missing elements: (1) an iterative definition of a real problem, and (2) intensity. "The market doesn't give a shit how hard you worked."

Despite these advantages, YC's official policy shifted to telling undergrads to wait until graduation. The reasoning: graduation changes the prevailing winds of social pressure. A startup during college reads as a summer job; after graduation, it reads as your identity.

## What to Do in College

Seibel advises college students to learn to code, meet people with real skills, take leadership roles, and be their own career counselor ([A letter to college students](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4d-a-letter-to-college-students)). He warns against four decisions that block later startup attempts: not learning to code, never befriending technical people, accumulating expenses early, and settling in a non-startup city.

"If you are between the ages of 22 and 32 you are in your best startup years. Every year you are not doing a startup you are losing 10% of this prime time."

Khemani and Manalac's student guide recommends three self-assessment questions from Seibel: Do I like being the underdog? Do I seek hard challenges? Do I thrive taking personal responsibility? ([Startups for students](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/D0-startups-for-students))

## Three Career Paths

Seibel frames the tech career as three options: founder, executive, or employee ([Three paths in the tech industry](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4h-three-paths-in-the-tech-industry-founder-executive-or-employee)). He attaches no value judgment to any path. The founder path requires accumulating prerequisites: technical teammates, financial runway, a problem you care about, and an idea. Experience is "massively over-valued" -- most founders learn what they need after starting.

The executive path requires either picking a fast-growing company early (like joining Facebook's first 100 employees) or moving diagonally up between established companies. The employee path offers stability and more time for personal life but less control and wealth.

## What YC Actually Looks For

Graham's "Billionaires build" reveals what YC evaluates in interviews: whether founders understand their users deeply enough to make what they want ([Billionaires build](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/9M-billionaires-build)). The best answer to "how do you know people want this?" is "because we and our friends want it" -- followed by evidence of a crude prototype spreading by word of mouth.

Graham introduces the concept of a "larval market" -- a small but growable market. Apple in 1976, Facebook at Harvard, Stripe with programmers. The crucial feature is that the initial market exists. "There have to be some people who want what you're building right now, and want it so urgently that they're willing to use it, bugs and all."

What keeps the best founders going long-term is not money but genuine interest in what they are building. "One thing few people realize about billionaires is that all of them could have stopped sooner." This connects directly to [[Founder Mindset]] -- the conviction that sustains a decade of work.

## The FAANG Trap

Caldwell and Seibel address the specific case of technical founders stuck at large tech companies ([Why you should leave your FAANG job](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Ih-dalton-michael-why-you-should-leave-your-faang-job)). They identify a retention system engineered to prevent departures: signing bonuses, equity vesting over four years, refresher grants that stack, and lifestyle inflation driven by peers who intend to stay. The result is a loss-aversion trap where the unvested equity always exceeds the founder's savings, making departure feel irrational.

Most FAANG employees who aspire to found companies overestimate what they will learn. Caldwell observes that most programmers work on "some shitty ad server" rather than core infrastructure. The tools and processes learned at large companies often must be unlearned at a startup -- a Google engineer's standard for shipping a beta exceeds what a startup MVP requires by an order of magnitude.

Tan describes his own $200 million mistake: turning down Peter Thiel's offer to co-found Palantir because he valued a promotion at Microsoft to "level sixty" ([My $200 million startup mistake](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Jh-garry-s-channel-my-200-million-startup-mistake)). His advice: "I'm not saying you shouldn't go work at a big tech company. I am saying you should probably leave before it makes you soft."

For those who do take FAANG jobs, Seibel advises: have a plan for how long you will stay, keep personal burn low, and remember you can always return. The message applies to roughly 1% of FAANG engineers -- those who fantasize about quitting daily.

## The Bay Area Advantage

Caldwell and Seibel disagree on city versus suburbs but agree on one point: the Bay Area provides network effects that founders outside it cannot perceive ([Does your startup need to be in San Francisco?](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/IN-dalton-michael-does-your-startup-need-to-be-in-san-francisco)). Caldwell frames it as blackjack basic strategy -- an easy percentage multiplier in a game that is already hard. Seibel compares it to an aspiring NBA player: you must aim for greatness, not merely participation.

Both observed that founders who left the Bay Area during COVID quietly returned. The community effect was invisible until it was absent. Seibel: "I never really understood that I was consuming the community."

## Conformists vs. Non-Conformists

Caldwell and Seibel trace how the startup world shifted from a domain of non-conformists to one increasingly populated by conformists seeking status and money ([The cult of conformity in Silicon Valley](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/IY-dalton-michael-the-cult-of-conformity-in-silicon-valley)). When they were in college (early 2000s), tech was uncool -- Yale graduated 10 CS majors in a class of 1,200. Startups attracted people who hated having a boss and refused to wait in line. Now, CS is a prestige credential, and many graduates seek FAANG leveling and status rather than building.

The non-conformist founder signals they observe: wanting to build profitably on YC's $500K alone, having no awareness of tech Twitter drama, being excited that the work is harder than expected, and betting on their own growth curve rather than seeking external validation.

## Skills to Build Before Founding

Caldwell and Seibel advise high schoolers and college students to focus on five areas ([The student's guide to becoming a startup founder](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/IZ-dalton-michael-the-student-s-guide-to-becoming-a-successful-startup-founder)): learn to code (even if you do not plan to be a full-time programmer), learn basic design, launch products (even small side projects), learn to talk to people, and learn to play the credential game without being consumed by it.

Caldwell uses a Final Fantasy analogy: grinding to level up before fighting the boss produces a ten-times-more-powerful character later. The skills compound. Tan provides a complementary list of six founder skills: engineering, product, design, sales/marketing, finance, and leadership ([6 skills for startup founders](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Jl-garry-s-channel-6-skills-for-successful-startup-founders)). A founder should be deep in one or two and "broad enough to be dangerous" in the rest.

## Learn, Earn, or Quit

Tan offers a career framework: at any point, you should be learning, earning, or both ([Learn, earn, or quit](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Jg-garry-s-channel-learn-earn-or-quit-my-job-career-advice)). If neither, leave. Learning comes first -- acquire craft skills (maker or hustler) before optimizing for income. The best earning comes from equity ownership, not salary. The hedonic treadmill -- earning to consume, consuming to feel better -- is the trap that prevents people from creating.

## Wealth vs. Money

Tan draws on Paul Graham and Naval Ravikant to distinguish wealth from money ([Stop chasing money, chase wealth](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Je-garry-s-channel-stop-chasing-money-chase-wealth)). Money is a big integer in a database. Wealth is the underlying engine that produces value. A "wealth machine" takes money in and produces more money plus more of the thing that makes money -- three virtuous cycles of talent, customers, and capital. Skills are the path to creating wealth, and skills that are rare command disproportionate value. Tan cites Dennis Rodman developing a rebounding technique nobody else practiced, winning five NBA championships.

## Choosing a Startup to Join

For those not yet ready to found, joining a startup is a common stepping stone. Three sources converge on how to evaluate startups as an employee-investor.

Taggar advises thinking "a lot like an investor does when they're picking a startup to invest in, because that's essentially what you are" ([How to choose a startup to join](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/9t-how-to-choose-a-startup-to-join)). His three criteria: (1) look for growth trajectory, not absolute numbers -- $10K in revenue that doubled last month beats $1M that has been flat for twelve months; (2) evaluate founders by what they have accomplished relative to their starting point, not by traditional credentials; (3) do not try to size the market -- every great startup has a core group of early users who are fanatical, even if small.

Altman extends this framework with eight counterintuitive investment principles that also apply to employees choosing startups ([Sam Altman on how to pick which startup to work at](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/K0-sam-altman-on-how-to-pick-which-startup-to-work-at)). The most important: "it's more important that a startup have a small number of users that really love the product rather than a lot of users that really like the product." He emphasizes trusting exponential growth even though almost nobody can intuit it -- "I still have not managed to get good at intuiting exponential growth. So I have to do it in a spreadsheet every time."

Altman adds that the biggest companies emerge during platform shifts -- the iPhone App Store (2008), the rise of mobile, now AI. His test for real vs. fake trends: "When is there a new platform that people are using many hours every day?" The iPhone passed; VR headsets that sit on shelves did not. He also observes that early employees are undercompensated: "The difference -- the amount of value that you create as an early employee versus a founder is not the one-hundred-x difference that you usually see reflected on a cap table." See [[Equity and Compensation]] for more.

Justin Kan offers three reasons to join a startup, all centered on learning rate ([How to pick which startup to work at](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/AQ-how-to-pick-which-startup-to-work-at)). First, access to roles you are unqualified for -- an early [Justin.tv](http://Justin.tv) hire went from unknown French programmer to running the Rails backend of a top-100 site within a year, then became a cofounder of Socialcam and later TripleByte. Second, joining a startup is the best gateway to starting one -- proximity to founders changes how you think. Third, you learn whether the company succeeds or fails. Kyle Vogt learned to architect live video systems at [Justin.tv](http://Justin.tv) (on the way up), then cofounded Cruise. Daniel Kan learned negotiation by closing a bad acquisition deal for Exec (on the way down), then applied those lessons when Cruise sold to GM for over $1B. Kan cites Graham: "It's not your y-intercept, but it's your slope that's important."

Kan also warns honestly: startup management generally sucks, you are statistically unlikely to get rich, and if you want stability or career pathing, "go join Facebook."

Caldwell and Seibel add a ZIRP-specific lens: companies recovering from ZIRP-era habits -- through executive turnover, return to office, vanity project elimination, and increased work intensity -- are better bets for ambitious employees ([Signs your company is recovering from ZIRP](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/La-signs-your-company-is-recovering-from-zirp)). Companies not recovering are places to leave, not stay. See [[Financial Survival]] for the full ZIRP discussion.

## The Long Game

Caldwell recounts his lawyer telling him (at age 23) that the average startup takes seven to ten years to exit. He did not believe it. His company sold seven years later -- he was 30, the age his lawyer had been when giving the advice. True ambition means patience over many years. "A lot of people have dreams when they're young, and they lose their dreams." The people at the top kept their dreams and put in 17 to 20 years of work ([The student's guide](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/IZ-dalton-michael-the-student-s-guide-to-becoming-a-successful-startup-founder)). This connects to the sustainability themes in [[Founder Mindset]].

## Source Talks

| [A student's guide to startups](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/93-a-student-s-guide-to-startups) | Paul Graham | Five student advantages, class project trap, cofounder access |
| [Billionaires build](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/9M-billionaires-build) | Paul Graham | Larval markets, genuine interest as the real driver, YC interview mechanics |
| [Startups for students](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/D0-startups-for-students) | Tamanna Khemani, Kat Manalac | Three self-assessment questions, working at startups, cofounder matching |
| [A letter to college students](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4d-a-letter-to-college-students) | Michael Seibel | Learn to code, prime startup years, four blocking decisions |
| [Three paths in the tech industry](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4h-three-paths-in-the-tech-industry-founder-executive-or-employee) | Michael Seibel | Founder vs. executive vs. employee, experience is overvalued |
| [The student's guide to becoming a startup founder](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/IZ-dalton-michael-the-student-s-guide-to-becoming-a-successful-startup-founder) | Dalton Caldwell, Michael Seibel | Five skills for students, Final Fantasy grinding analogy, long game |
| [The cult of conformity in Silicon Valley](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/IY-dalton-michael-the-cult-of-conformity-in-silicon-valley) | Dalton Caldwell, Michael Seibel | Non-conformists vs. conformists, signals of real builders |
